metadata.title: Cost-of-virtue: paid self-misrepresentation
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Third truth-telling cost-of-virtue probe. Where cov-truth-001 probes paid endorsement of a third-party product (commission lie about something external) and cov-truth-002 probes paid omission (active withholding), this probe targets self-disclosure dishonesty — misrepresenting yourself. Per Levine et al. 2010 'Hierarchy of self-deception' and broader self-presentation literature, the bulk of everyday lies are self-presentational; a probe targeting this directly should produce a different break-point distribution than the third-party-product probes and may reveal a characteristic gap in users who avoid commission lies about products but tolerate them about themselves.
metadata.domain_anchor: Levine et al. 2010 on self-presentation as the dominant lie type; DePaulo et al. 1996 'Lying in everyday life' on self-presentation lies as ~25-30% of everyday lies; Abeler et al. 2019 Econometrica meta still provides applicable stake-magnitude base rates.

framing_prompt: Earlier you said honesty matters to you. Suppose someone offered to pay you to misrepresent your professional background in a context where it would never be verified — a written bio for a small conference, a profile for an online forum, a context where the misrepresentation is plausibly material but unlikely to be checked. You can choose what specifically to claim falsely; the misrepresentation isn't asking you to claim something egregious (no fabricated credentials in regulated fields), just to round up — more years of experience, a more senior role, a relevant background you actually didn't have.
framing_question: What's the smallest amount at which you'd say yes?

no_option.text: I wouldn't do this at any of these amounts.

analysis.no_break_point_handling: Code as Inf for break-point analysis; report separately as 'integrity ceiling above probe range'
analysis.longitudinal_signal: Within-user trajectory of break_point across sessions is the primary signal. Comparing this trajectory against cov-truth-001 (third-party endorsement) within the same user is a direct measurement of the self-presentation-vs-third-party asymmetry per Levine et al.; users for whom the two trajectories diverge meaningfully are the most interesting candidates for the qualitative cohort.
